title: Thread and silk inclusions in handmade paper
subtitle: Embedding decorative elements into a sheet during the forming stage
excerpt: Threads, silk fibres, dried petals, and torn fabric can be embedded in a handmade sheet during forming, becoming a permanent part of the paper rather than a surface decoration. The inclusion must be placed at the right moment in the forming process to sit within the fibre layer rather than on top of it.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, public domain via Internet Archive), on decorative inclusions in Asian and European decorative papers, including the use of silk, mica, and coloured threads in Japanese and Florentine paper traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorative element embedded within a handmade sheet during forming, held in place by the surrounding fibre as the sheet drains and dries.

A forming technique in which the mould is lifted halfway out of the vat, the inclusions are placed on the partially drained fibre mat, and then the mould is dipped and lifted again to trap the inclusions within the fibre layer.
The alignment between successive sheets on the couching cloths, maintained so that the post can be pressed evenly without sheets slipping.

content.text: must sit within the sheet, not on top of it. A thread or petal laid on a formed sheet before couching stays on the surface and peels off when dry. The correct method uses the
technique to sandwich the inclusion inside two layers of fibre.

attrs.heading: What works as an inclusion
attrs.body: Short silk fibres, linen threads, dried petals (press first to remove moisture), torn washi fibres, mica flakes, and coloured cotton embroidery thread all embed well. Avoid inclusions that will rot (fresh plant matter) or bleed (unstabilised fabric dyes).

items.name: Cotton linter or kozo pulp
items.notes: A translucent fibre like kozo shows inclusions to best effect.

items.name: Thread or silk inclusions
items.notes: Cut silk thread to 10 to 20 mm lengths. Dried pressed petals should be flattened and completely dry.

items.name: Papermaking vat, sponge, press boards, couching cloths

content.text: Pull the mould from the vat in the normal way and allow it to drain for ten seconds until the surface water has mostly drained but the fibre mat is still very wet. Place the inclusions on the wet fibre mat: lay threads in a considered arrangement, scatter petals loosely, or arrange silk fibres in a direction that follows the sheet. Then gently lower the mould back into the vat at a slight angle and lift it again immediately in a smooth, level pull. The second pull deposits a thin second layer of fibre over the inclusions, trapping them inside the sheet.
Couch and press as normal, maintaining

through the post. Sheets with heavy inclusions like petals may need longer pressing because the inclusion body holds moisture. Dry flat or board-dry; inclusions near the edges can cause the sheet to cockle if one area dries faster than the rest.

items.symptom: Inclusions sit on the surface and peel off when dry
items.cause: The second pull was too thin or the inclusions were added too late in the drain.
items.fix: Add inclusions earlier, when the mat is still very wet. Pull more firmly on the second lift to deposit a thicker fibre layer over them.

items.symptom: The sheet is very thick and uneven around the inclusions
items.fix: Use thinner inclusions (shorter thread lengths, thinner silk strands, flatter petals). Thick inclusions create a ridge in the sheet that doesn't press flat.
